item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the spooky chocolate cult, and the lack of them sacrificing the children for pure horrors sake, and instead going down the perhaps more horrifying smart option. The only sentence that tripped me up was this sentence here:

D-Class that consumed the chocolate during testing reported that it is of high quality and good taste before being infected by SCP-3409-A.

It isn't exactly grammatically or structurally wrong, but it is slightly unclear on first reading as to whether the D-Class get infected by SCP-3409-A. If they do, then how do the children later in the article eat the chocolate and remain fine? Is it different chocolate? If this sentence is saying that the D-Class who sampled the chocolate became infected, maybe :

D-Class that consumed the chocolate during testing reported that it was of high quality and good taste, before they were subsequent;y infected by SCP-3409-A.

Would be more clear. Anyways that's just a minor nitpick, I liked it overall, especially the 3D MRI. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the tips and points, I will be sure to keep them in mind in future, or if the article needs some editing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, the base idea isn't bad but there are several issues that kept me from being able to enjoy it. Most have to do with the Containment and Description portions. Overall, they seem… Well, cluttered and unprofessional. I mean, what sort of self-respecting researcher would use the phrase "chocolate-like substance"? Use more clinical terms. For example:

SCP-3409-A is an anomolous fungoid organism appearing dark brown to black with a "waxy" or "glossy" texture.

Speaking of designations, you seem to inconsistently use "SCP-3409" to refer to the building itself vs. the contents of said building. It'd be better if you gave the building proper its own letter designation; the victims, the rift, and the chocolate world all probably deserve one as well (taking you all the way up to SCP-3409-F).

Your containment proceedures are in need of an overhaul: you can't just "hermetically seal" a pre-existing structure. It'd be more realistic (and more interesting to the reader) to say that some environment-containing structure was built around the SCP (maybe including an artificial foundation ██m underground). Also, don't put any sort of justification inside the containment proceedures; if the readers can't figure out the reasoning after reading the entire article, then it shouldn't be necessary to understand the SCP. Oh, and include a line about patrolling the perimeter and managing interior growth with a fungicide (that actually works; we have enough "cannot be stopped" SCPs).

Finally, you should probably be utilizing redaction more. For instance:

Interrogation of the subjects revealed them to be associated with [REDACTED].

Redacting there leaves a hole for the reader to fill in with his own imagination, leaving a stronger impression. Oh, and the Eldrich Names in the interview really need to be [REDACTED]; it breaks immersion since the Foundation wouldn't leave such an obvious potential cognitohazard unredacted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was expecting a Willy Wonka reference here, or maybe a transference of it into Foundation canon/whatever you wish to call it, but I love this infinitely more.

I admit I chuckled about the 'chocolate gods' bit at first, then I heard about the sacrificing and immediately felt my heart sink. (I am light-hearted, perhaps…) Especially keeping kids and raising them just to be sacrificed.

I even expected a link to 743 in some fashion, but I'm happy that they're their own separate anomalies.

I have nothing else to say other than that this is an awesome blend in humor and dark tone, and I upvote this wholeheartedly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The following is my interpretation of the situation. It's not included in the articles, because I want it to be ambiguous, and for readers to come up with their own interpretations. But if you'd like to know what I was thinking when I was writing this, I included it below. Feel free to skip this if you want it to remain a mystery.

+ My interpretation of what's going on
- hide

Setting

This actually takes place after the events of SCP-3974. Garrison (SCP-3974-B) has broken free of containment, and established a cult, through use of charisma, and perhaps mind-altering chocolate where necessary.

Spacial anomaly

What you found within the temple is an incomplete, false gateway to the chocolate realm. Home of the chocolate gods, our gracious overlords.

In Interview Log 3409-1, the POI indicates the the 'portal' is incomplete and false; it can only allow certain entities to pass through, ie the fungus. This is because they require four objects to open the true gateway, which are in possession of the Foundation. Once this gateway is opened, any organism from the other side can pass through.

How does it exist? I'd say its held open because of the incantations chanted by the caged humans.

'Deities' mentioned

Narzeil'lamarg is the chocolate 'god' of parasitic organisms; the spawn of Shimbul'liera, chocolate 'goddess' of life, and an unnamed 'deity', the chocolate 'god' of disease. It is in the interests of the cultists to awaken this being, because it can pass through the portal, because it possesses the blood of its mother, which exists as the fungus. They believe that the being of interest can give them enough force to retrieve the objects they require, ie the four chocolate children.

Even if they did awaken it, however, it wouldn't exist in its full form. It would still exist as viruses and parasites at most, but it would be enough to cause a widespread plague among civilian populations.

It is capable of telepathy, able to see through any parasitic organism it creates. In SCP-3974, it is likely controlling the entity that spawned out of SCP-3974-B's body, and was attempting to retrieve the objects of interest, but was destroyed by the Foundation.

Are the chocolate gods really deities? Probably not. Rather, I imagine the entities to be vast organisms that are so long-lived that they may be considered immortal by human standards, and part of some ecosystem that feeds off the nutrients of other worlds. They gain access to these worlds by influencing and targeting intelligent species that have the means to give it to them.

Cultists' situation

  1. They can't awaken Shimbul'liera because is currently dormant. It intervened to save its spawn from being destroyed by 'false gods', by breaking it up into small fragments they couldn't detect, ie viruses. In doing so, it expended a big portion of its energy, and would allow its spawn to continue living. This is the same virus involved in SCP-3974.
  2. The being known as Narzeil'lamarg is still weakened, and requires human sacrifice. Its influence and intelligence is directly proportional to the number of people it infects.
  3. The means to contact other 'deities' is currently beyond their reach.

The caged humans

The humans are affected by a hallucinogenic compound produced by the fungus, which blocks out pain and induces a heightened state of spirituality, but shuts off many parts of the brain so they may 'babble', but I like to think they're actually being manipulated to speak in the tongue of the 'deities'.

The reason why this entity requires human sacrifice, is that it uses nutrients and tissues from terrestrial organisms to rebuild itself.

It will rebuild itself in the following stages:

  1. Viruses
  2. Parasitic organisms, there are its individual cells
  3. Greater versions of the previous parasites (SCP-3409-2), this will become its organs
  4. Complete organism; true form

The fungus absorbs nutrients from the terrestrial world, and transfers it back to 'the chocolate realm', so the leeches observed can absorb it, and eventually reform the entity known as Narzeil'lamarg.

They aim to acquire more sacrifices by distributing the fungal chocolate to civilian populations to infect large groups of people.

What's the chocolate realm?

I was inspired by the movie, The Mist. I imagine it to be a form of 'Todash Space', spaces between reality where any form of organism imaginable may potentially exist.

Maybe it's another version of Earth, where the 'chocolate gods' already sucked it dry.

Either way, it could actually have been another world that was sucked dry. the chocolate 'gods' influence an entity, or entities, remotely (in this case, Garrison), so they will let them in. They get access to the world, drain it of nutrients, and repeat the cycle by contacting another world.

The vehicles and bones seen within the space may be the result of weakening of reality in certain areas at various points in time. The structures seen probably faded out of existence in this world, and faded into existence on the other side, where the organisms there preyed upon the victims. Alternatively, it could be the remnants of another Earth.

What do the tattoos mean

The tattoos on the cultists indicate the cacao tree as the 'giver of chocolate', and the three rings represent the eldest of the means through which the 'chocolate message' spreads, which are disease (bacterial and viral infection), fungal infection, and parasitic infection.

Why chocolate?

I wanted to play on juxtaposition of ideas and association; chocolate is nice, demonic beings and cults aren't. I think I was also inspired by the Chocolate Fountain SCP. Suffice it to say that I love chocolate!

Also, I stumbled upon some fan theory of the character, Willy Wonka, from Charlie and the Chocolate Factory, being evil. Needless to say I got my own idea from it and had to put it in writing.

End

There's more to the story, but I'll leave it here for now.

All in all, this is just my interpretation, so it never happened yet in-universe. I left out any mention of it because I think it's better for the reader to imagine what's going on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you very much, glad you enjoyed the read.

I really wanted to expand on the first one, so I decided to write this separate article that's linked to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure, please do, thank you for the help.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First chocolate children, now chocolate fungus. These chocolate gods work fast!

I think I like this article better than the first one, those life form descriptions were especially unsettling. But it's bolstered by the connection to the first, so that's a good thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was close to upvoting, this close, but my inner grammar nazi held me back.

"The means to which the fungus achieves this" (grammar) to -> by
"Advanced stages of infection results" (subj/verb agreement) stages … result
"Testing on D-Class personnel indicate … and the subject may expire" (subj/verb agreement) testing … indicates / subjects may expire
"36 cages are occupied with live, adult humans"
And more.

Mind if I edit?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much, I was hoping to achieve that effect. Glad you enjoyed the read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for your help, glad you enjoyed the read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This gets a little long in the tooth in some places but I think it strikes a nice balance between amusing and unsettling. Concept is a bit broad but it's not to the articles detriment and there's some nice detail here and there. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this article pretty well as a draft, and I definitely still enjoy it now.

The concept of a chocolate cult is amusing and bizarre. It reminds me a little of some episodes from my favorite cartoon.

Nice work.

+1

item.title: Introduction
item.content:encoded:
This is an off-beat article, involving a cult, a spacial anomaly, and a chocolate factory.

Hope you like it.

Big thanks to rioludoodle for the feedback on the draft.

Edit: Made an image for the article using Autodesk Mudbox 2017.
